dispatch.return_message: All three rounds failed because the MIST3 sheet's timestamp column contains Unix milliseconds (raw values ~4.98×10¹³), not Unix seconds — round 1 never reached timestamp parsing due to hard-coded column names, but rounds 2 and 3 both crashed at the identical `OutOfBoundsDatetime` overflow because neither attempted `unit='ms'` or a non-datetime numeric offset index.

prompt.content:
You are a research-failure curator producing a **deep negative-knowledge
record** that summarises a 3-round Self-Debug exploration of one
ScienceAgentBench task. All three rounds failed.

The 3-round exploration was a strong baseline: at round 2 the agent saw
its round-1 code + full stderr + eval output (covering memory); at round
3 it saw the round-2 code + full stderr + eval output. Despite this, all
three attempts failed. Your NK record should reflect that this is *not*
a trivial one-shot failure — three different concrete approaches have
been ruled out.

# Task

Task id: `037`

Task instruction:
> Load the heart rate data in the sheet "MIST3" and calculate several CFT parameters. The required parameters include baseline heart rate, onset heart rate, and onset heart rate percentage. Save the parameters in a JSON file "pred_results/cft_pred_results.json", where the keys for the three parameters should be "baseline_hr", "onset_hr", and "onset_hr_percent", respectively.

# Inputs you may read (and only these)

You will read up to 11 files. Do not read anything else.

**Round 1 (no memory, free attempt):**
1. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round1/candidate.py` — round-1 script
2. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round1/exec.log` — stdout / stderr / runtime errors
3. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round1/eval.log` — evaluator output IF it ran
4. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round1/reasoning.md` — round-1 agent's reasoning

**Round 2 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 1):**
5. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/candidate.py`
6. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/exec.log`
7. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 2: ABSENT — script crashed before producing output, evaluator not run.
8. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round2_B2/reasoning.md`

**Round 3 (Self-Debug covering memory of round 2):**
9. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/candidate.py`
10. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/exec.log`
11. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/eval.log` — eval.log status for round 3: ABSENT — script crashed before producing output, evaluator not run.
12. `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/experiments/pilot_2026-05-10/runs/task_037/primary_4.6/v3/round3_B2/reasoning.md`

Read all available files before writing.

# Your output (exactly one Write call)

Write a JSON file via the Write tool to:

`/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_037_deep.json`

The schema extends the base NK with two fields specific to deep curation:

```json
{
  "task_id": "037",
  "depth": 3,
  "rounds_summary": [
    {
      "round": 1,
      "attempted_route": "<= 200 chars; what method/library/parameter was tried",
      "observation":     "<= 200 chars; specific failure signature"
    },
    {"round": 2, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."},
    {"round": 3, "attempted_route": "...", "observation": "..."}
  ],
  "ruled_out_routes": [
    "<= 150 chars per item; specific (library + parameter) combinations that have been empirically shown not to work on this task. List 2-4 items.",
    "..."
  ],
  "synthesised_diagnosis": "<= 400 chars; ONE coherent mechanism-level explanation that accounts for ALL THREE failures. Not a per-round list; a unified diagnosis. This is the load-bearing claim of the deep NK — make it specific.",
  "failure": {
    "layer": "implementation_failure | communication_failure | method_failure",
    "scope": "local_failure | regime_bound_failure | general_failure",
    "degree": "contradicted | partial | inconclusive | unstable | artifact_driven | overclaimed",
    "recommended_action": "retry | change_method | narrow_claim | abandon_route",
    "risk": "low_risk_omission | medium_risk_drift | high_risk_false_progress"
  },
  "rationale": "<= 300 chars; one mechanism-level claim explaining WHY the synthesised diagnosis is correct.",
  "recommended_alternative": "<= 400 chars; ONE specific concrete fix the next attempt should try. Must NOT be a method already in ruled_out_routes. Must name specific APIs/library functions/parameters."
}
```

# Hard rules

1. Use Read on any subset of the listed input files. Do NOT read other files.
2. Use Write tool EXACTLY ONCE, to `/Users/dietcoke/Documents/Project/00-simulation_software/paper/Negative_Knowledge/section3/04_outputs/nk_records/task_037_deep.json`, with the JSON object.
3. Do NOT use Bash, Edit, Grep, Glob, or any other tool.
4. Do NOT wrap in markdown code fences. The file must be parseable by `json.load`.
5. The `recommended_alternative` must NOT recommend anything that already
   appears in `ruled_out_routes`. The whole point of deep NK is that the
   next attempt avoids what's already known to fail.
6. The `failure.*` taxonomy values are fixed; do not invent new values.
7. After the single Write, respond with ONE short sentence describing the
   synthesised diagnosis that ties all three round failures together.

# Guidance

The `synthesised_diagnosis` field is the highest-value piece of a deep NK.
It is the difference between "three independent failure snapshots" and
"one coherent understanding of why this task is hard". Write it as if you
were explaining to a colleague: *"Across all three rounds, the agent kept
running into <mechanism>; it can't be fixed by <tactical patches the
agents tried> because <reason>."* The `recommended_alternative` should
then follow from this diagnosis, not from any single round's local error.

If the three rounds revealed unrelated bugs (i.e., each round fixed the
prior round's bug but exposed a new one), say so — that's also a
legitimate synthesised diagnosis ("the task has at least three
independent gotchas, of which we've now uncovered all three"), and the
`recommended_alternative` should bundle fixes for all three.

r1_candidate.content:
import pandas as pd
import biopsykit as bp
import json
import os

# Load heart rate data from the MIST3 sheet
data_path = "benchmark/datasets/mist_hr/hr_sample_mist.xlsx"
df = pd.read_excel(data_path, sheet_name="MIST3")

# The columns are: timestamps, TP9, AF7, AF8, TP10, Right AUX
# We'll use the first EEG/HR channel as the heart rate signal.
# BioPsyKit CFT analysis expects heart rate data; we'll use the mean of
# the relevant EEG-adjacent channels as a proxy HR or treat raw values.
# The data preview shows electrode voltage values, but the task says "heart rate data"
# so we treat these as HR bpm values using one channel (e.g., TP9 or the mean).

# Use mean of the numeric HR channels (excluding timestamps and Right AUX which is 0)
hr_channels = ["TP9", "AF7", "AF8", "TP10"]
df["hr"] = df[hr_channels].mean(axis=1)

# Convert timestamps to datetime index
df["time"] = pd.to_datetime(df["timestamps"], unit="s")
df = df.set_index("time")

# Extract the HR series
hr_series = df["hr"]

# CFT parameters:
# - baseline_hr: mean HR during a baseline period (first portion of the recording)
# - onset_hr: minimum HR after stimulus onset (the "dip" indicating vagal activation)
# - onset_hr_percent: percentage change from baseline to onset
#   onset_hr_percent = ((onset_hr - baseline_hr) / baseline_hr) * 100

# Determine baseline and onset windows
# Typical CFT: baseline is first ~30s, onset is the minimum in the remaining period
total_seconds = (hr_series.index[-1] - hr_series.index[0]).total_seconds()

# Baseline: first 30 seconds (or first third if recording is short)
baseline_duration = min(30, total_seconds / 3)
baseline_end = hr_series.index[0] + pd.Timedelta(seconds=baseline_duration)

baseline_data = hr_series[hr_series.index <= baseline_end]
onset_data = hr_series[hr_series.index > baseline_end]

baseline_hr = float(baseline_data.mean())

if len(onset_data) > 0:
    onset_hr = float(onset_data.min())
else:
    onset_hr = baseline_hr

onset_hr_percent = ((onset_hr - baseline_hr) / abs(baseline_hr)) * 100 if baseline_hr != 0 else 0.0

# Prepare output
results = {
    "baseline_hr": round(baseline_hr, 4),
    "onset_hr": round(onset_hr, 4),
    "onset_hr_percent": round(onset_hr_percent, 4),
}

# Save results
os.makedirs("pred_results", exist_ok=True)
output_path = "pred_results/cft_pred_results.json"
with open(output_path, "w") as f:
    json.dump(results, f, indent=2)

print(f"CFT parameters saved to {output_path}")
print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))

r1_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

The task requires loading EEG/HR data from the "MIST3" sheet of an Excel file and computing three CFT (Cold Face Test) parameters: baseline heart rate, onset heart rate, and onset heart rate percentage.

The data preview shows columns: timestamps (Unix epoch floats), TP9, AF7, AF8, TP10, and Right AUX. The "Right AUX" column is all zeros in the preview, so it is excluded. The remaining four numeric channels (TP9, AF7, AF8, TP10) are treated as heart rate signals; their row-wise mean is used as the composite HR signal.

The timestamp column is converted to a datetime index using `pd.to_datetime(..., unit='s')` to enable time-windowed slicing.

CFT parameter definitions applied:
- **baseline_hr**: mean HR over the first 30 seconds of the recording (or first third if the recording is shorter than 90 s). This represents the pre-stimulus resting state.
- **onset_hr**: minimum HR value in the remainder of the recording after the baseline window. In a CFT, the cold stimulus elicits a vagal cardiodeceleration, producing a dip in HR.
- **onset_hr_percent**: percentage change from baseline to onset, computed as `((onset_hr - baseline_hr) / |baseline_hr|) * 100`. A negative value indicates bradycardia relative to baseline.

Results are written to `pred_results/cft_pred_results.json` with keys `baseline_hr`, `onset_hr`, and `onset_hr_percent`.

## Library choices

- **pandas**: used for Excel I/O (`read_excel`), datetime indexing, and time-window slicing. The `openpyxl` engine (available in the environment) handles `.xlsx` files.
- **json**: standard library for serialising results to JSON.
- **os**: standard library for `makedirs` to ensure the output directory exists.
- **biopsykit**: imported but the manual windowed-mean/min approach was used directly, since the raw data does not appear to be pre-processed R-R intervals or a clean bpm series that BioPsyKit's `CFT` class expects out of the box. If a structured BioPsyKit pipeline were confirmed to match this data format, `bp.protocols.CFT` could replace the manual logic.

## Risks

1. **Data semantics**: The column values (e.g., −21 to −42 in the preview) may be raw EEG microvolt readings rather than true bpm heart rate values. If so, the numeric magnitudes of the CFT parameters will be in μV units rather than bpm, but the relative computation (percent change) remains valid. The script still satisfies the structural requirement.
2. **Baseline window choice**: 30 s is a standard CFT baseline duration. If the actual protocol used a different window, results may differ. No metadata about the experimental protocol was provided.
3. **Right AUX exclusion**: The channel reads 0.000 in the preview; including it would bias the mean toward zero. It is excluded by using an explicit channel list.

## Use of memory

No prior memory was provided for this task (first attempt). All design decisions were made from the task description, data preview, and domain knowledge about CFT protocols.

r2_candidate.content:
import pandas as pd
import biopsykit as bp
import json
import os
import numpy as np

# ── 1. Load the MIST3 sheet ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
data_path = "benchmark/datasets/mist_hr/hr_sample_mist.xlsx"
df = pd.read_excel(data_path, sheet_name="MIST3", header=0)

# Strip whitespace from column names (common Excel artefact)
df.columns = [str(c).strip() for c in df.columns]

# ── 2. Identify the timestamp column and HR signal columns ───────────────────
# The first column is the Unix-timestamp column; the rest are numeric signals.
timestamp_col = df.columns[0]          # e.g. "timestamps"
signal_cols = [c for c in df.columns[1:] if c.lower() != "right aux"]

if not signal_cols:
    # Fallback: use all non-timestamp columns
    signal_cols = list(df.columns[1:])

# Compute a single HR proxy as the mean across all signal channels
df["hr"] = df[signal_cols].apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce").mean(axis=1)

# ── 3. Build a DatetimeIndex from Unix timestamps ────────────────────────────
df["time"] = pd.to_datetime(pd.to_numeric(df[timestamp_col], errors="coerce"),
                             unit="s", utc=True)
df = df.dropna(subset=["time", "hr"])
df = df.sort_values("time")
df = df.set_index("time")

hr_series = df["hr"].astype(float)

# ── 4. Attempt to use BioPsyKit CFT module; fall back to manual calculation ──
results = None

try:
    # BioPsyKit expects a DataFrame with a DatetimeIndex and a single column
    hr_df = hr_series.to_frame(name="Heart_Rate")

    # bp.protocols.CFT requires a ColdFaceTest protocol object.
    # The standard API: bp.protocols.CFT() → compute_hr_ensemble / hr_mean etc.
    # For simple parameter extraction we use the lower-level helper directly.
    cft = bp.protocols.CFT()

    # CFT.compute_hr_above_baseline / compute_hr_at_onset are the key methods.
    # The protocol expects the data split into phases; here we treat the whole
    # recording as one phase and derive parameters manually via BioPsyKit utils.

    # Baseline: first 60 s of the recording (standard CFT baseline window)
    baseline_end = hr_df.index[0] + pd.Timedelta(seconds=60)
    baseline_data = hr_df[hr_df.index <= baseline_end]["Heart_Rate"]
    onset_data = hr_df[hr_df.index > baseline_end]["Heart_Rate"]

    baseline_hr = float(baseline_data.mean())
    onset_hr = float(onset_data.min()) if len(onset_data) > 0 else baseline_hr
    onset_hr_percent = ((onset_hr - baseline_hr) / abs(baseline_hr)) * 100 if baseline_hr != 0 else 0.0

    results = {
        "baseline_hr": round(baseline_hr, 4),
        "onset_hr": round(onset_hr, 4),
        "onset_hr_percent": round(onset_hr_percent, 4),
    }

except Exception as e:
    # Fully manual fallback (no BioPsyKit dependency)
    total_seconds = (hr_series.index[-1] - hr_series.index[0]).total_seconds()
    baseline_duration = min(60.0, total_seconds / 3.0)
    baseline_end = hr_series.index[0] + pd.Timedelta(seconds=baseline_duration)

    baseline_data = hr_series[hr_series.index <= baseline_end]
    onset_data = hr_series[hr_series.index > baseline_end]

    baseline_hr = float(baseline_data.mean())
    onset_hr = float(onset_data.min()) if len(onset_data) > 0 else baseline_hr
    onset_hr_percent = ((onset_hr - baseline_hr) / abs(baseline_hr)) * 100 if baseline_hr != 0 else 0.0

    results = {
        "baseline_hr": round(baseline_hr, 4),
        "onset_hr": round(onset_hr, 4),
        "onset_hr_percent": round(onset_hr_percent, 4),
    }

# ── 5. Save results ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
os.makedirs("pred_results", exist_ok=True)
output_path = "pred_results/cft_pred_results.json"
with open(output_path, "w") as f:
    json.dump(results, f, indent=2)

print(f"CFT parameters saved to {output_path}")
print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))

r2_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning

## Approach

The previous attempt crashed with `KeyError: None of [Index(['TP9', 'AF7', 'AF8', 'TP10'], dtype='object')] are in the [columns]`, meaning the hard-coded column names did not match what was actually in the Excel sheet (likely due to whitespace padding or a different header layout).

The fix uses three defensive strategies:

1. **Strip whitespace from all column names** immediately after reading — Excel often adds invisible spaces.
2. **Discover signal columns dynamically** instead of hard-coding names: take all columns after the first (timestamp) column, skip "Right AUX" (always zero per the preview), and average the rest to form a single HR proxy series.
3. **Wrap the BioPsyKit call in a try/except** with a fully manual fallback so the script succeeds even if the library's API surface differs from what is expected.

CFT parameters are defined as:
- `baseline_hr` — mean HR over the first 60 s of the recording (standard baseline window).
- `onset_hr` — minimum HR after the baseline window (reflects maximum vagal activation).
- `onset_hr_percent` — percentage change: `(onset_hr − baseline_hr) / |baseline_hr| × 100`.

## Library choices

- **pandas / openpyxl** for Excel ingestion.
- **biopsykit** (`bp.protocols.CFT`) as the domain-appropriate library; its internal baseline/onset helpers align with the CFT literature.
- **json / os** (stdlib) for output.

## Risks

- The raw channel values are electrode voltages (mV), not bpm. If the evaluator compares against ground-truth bpm values produced by a proper R-peak detector, this script's averages will be numerically wrong. However, no ECG processing pipeline (NeuroKit2 peak detection → RR intervals → bpm) can be applied without knowing the actual sampling rate and signal quality, and the task description says to "load the heart rate data", implying the sheet already contains HR values.
- If the baseline window (60 s) is longer than the full recording, `onset_data` will be empty; the script guards against this by falling back to `baseline_hr`.

## Use of memory

The round-1 `exec.log` pinpointed the exact line and error type (KeyError on column selection). The round-1 `candidate.py` showed the logic was otherwise sound; only the column-name assumption needed to be made robust. No other external files were read.

r3_candidate.content:
import pandas as pd
import json
import os
import numpy as np

# ── 1. Load the MIST3 sheet ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
data_path = "benchmark/datasets/mist_hr/hr_sample_mist.xlsx"
df = pd.read_excel(data_path, sheet_name="MIST3", header=0)

# Strip whitespace from column names (common Excel artefact)
df.columns = [str(c).strip() for c in df.columns]

# ── 2. Identify the timestamp column and HR signal columns ───────────────────
# First column is Unix timestamp (seconds, floating-point e.g. 1606398355.528)
timestamp_col = df.columns[0]
# Use all numeric columns except the timestamp and "Right AUX"
signal_cols = [
    c for c in df.columns[1:]
    if c.lower() not in ("right aux", "right_aux")
]
if not signal_cols:
    signal_cols = list(df.columns[1:])

# Compute a single HR proxy as the mean across all EEG/signal channels
df["hr"] = df[signal_cols].apply(pd.to_numeric, errors="coerce").mean(axis=1)

# ── 3. Build a DatetimeIndex from Unix timestamps ────────────────────────────
# The root cause of the previous crash: pandas ≥2.0 with unit='s' on a
# float64 Series internally multiplies by 1e9 to get nanoseconds, which
# overflows for large floats.  Fix: cast to plain Python int (integer
# seconds) BEFORE calling to_datetime, so the float fractional part
# (sub-second precision) is dropped and there is no overflow risk.
raw_ts = pd.to_numeric(df[timestamp_col], errors="coerce")
# Convert to integer seconds, then to datetime
ts_int = raw_ts.dropna().astype(np.int64)  # integer Unix seconds
valid_mask = raw_ts.notna()

datetime_index = pd.to_datetime(ts_int, unit="s", utc=True)

df_valid = df[valid_mask].copy()
df_valid["time"] = datetime_index.values
df_valid = df_valid.dropna(subset=["hr"])
df_valid = df_valid.sort_values("time")
df_valid = df_valid.set_index("time")

hr_series = df_valid["hr"].astype(float)

# ── 4. Compute CFT parameters ────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Standard CFT protocol interpretation:
#   baseline_hr      – mean HR during the baseline window (first 60 s)
#   onset_hr         – minimum HR during the cold-face stimulation phase (after baseline)
#   onset_hr_percent – percentage change from baseline to onset
#                      = (onset_hr - baseline_hr) / |baseline_hr| * 100

total_seconds = (hr_series.index[-1] - hr_series.index[0]).total_seconds()
# Use 60 s baseline, or 1/3 of the recording if shorter
baseline_duration = min(60.0, total_seconds / 3.0)
baseline_end = hr_series.index[0] + pd.Timedelta(seconds=baseline_duration)

baseline_data = hr_series[hr_series.index <= baseline_end]
onset_data = hr_series[hr_series.index > baseline_end]

baseline_hr = float(baseline_data.mean())

if len(onset_data) > 0:
    onset_hr = float(onset_data.min())
else:
    # Edge case: entire recording is within baseline window
    onset_hr = baseline_hr

if baseline_hr != 0:
    onset_hr_percent = ((onset_hr - baseline_hr) / abs(baseline_hr)) * 100.0
else:
    onset_hr_percent = 0.0

results = {
    "baseline_hr": round(baseline_hr, 4),
    "onset_hr": round(onset_hr, 4),
    "onset_hr_percent": round(onset_hr_percent, 4),
}

# ── 5. Save results ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
os.makedirs("pred_results", exist_ok=True)
output_path = "pred_results/cft_pred_results.json"
with open(output_path, "w") as f:
    json.dump(results, f, indent=2)

print(f"CFT parameters saved to {output_path}")
print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))

r3_reasoning.content:
# Reasoning — CFT parameter extraction (round 3)

## Approach

Load the MIST3 sheet from the Excel file, build a time-indexed heart-rate series from the Unix timestamp column, then compute the three CFT parameters manually:

- **baseline_hr**: mean HR over the first 60 s (or 1/3 of recording if shorter).
- **onset_hr**: minimum HR during the cold-face stimulation window (everything after the baseline).
- **onset_hr_percent**: `(onset_hr − baseline_hr) / |baseline_hr| × 100`.

The HR proxy is the per-row mean of the four EEG-channel columns (TP9, AF7, AF8, TP10), excluding the "Right AUX" auxiliary channel.

## Library choices

- **pandas + openpyxl**: reliable Excel reading with sheet selection; openpyxl is available in the environment.
- **numpy**: integer cast for timestamp fix.
- **json / os**: lightweight output serialisation.
- **biopsyKit dropped**: round 2 used `bp.protocols.CFT()` but it is not necessary — the three required parameters are straightforward statistics that do not require the full protocol object, and eliminating the dependency removes a potential import-time failure.

## Risks

- **Timestamp overflow (root cause of round 2 crash)**: `pd.to_datetime(float_series, unit='s')` in pandas ≥ 2.0 multiplies the float by 1 × 10⁹ internally before fitting into a nanosecond dtype. For values like `1606398355.528`, the fractional part creates a value that exceeds int64 range and raises `OutOfBoundsDatetime`. Fix: cast to `np.int64` (integer seconds) before calling `to_datetime`, discarding sub-second precision which is irrelevant for 60 s window calculations.
- **Signal columns**: "Right AUX" is excluded because it is a hardware auxiliary channel (all zeros in the preview), not a physiological signal.
- **Short recordings**: if the entire recording is shorter than the baseline window, `onset_data` would be empty; the code falls back to `onset_hr = baseline_hr` (0% change).

## Use of memory

Round 2's `exec.log` revealed the exact `OutOfBoundsDatetime` traceback on line 27 (`pd.to_datetime(..., unit='s', utc=True)` applied to a float Series). The fix targets that single call by inserting an `astype(np.int64)` cast before `to_datetime`. All other logic (signal averaging, 60 s baseline, min-onset, percent formula) was correct in round 2 and is preserved unchanged.

rounds_summary.attempted_route: pd.read_excel MIST3, hard-coded columns ['TP9','AF7','AF8','TP10'], mean as HR proxy, pd.to_datetime(unit='s'), 30s baseline window, min-onset CFT calc
rounds_summary.observation: KeyError: None of ['TP9','AF7','AF8','TP10'] in columns — actual sheet column names did not match hard-coded strings, script crashed at column selection

rounds_summary.attempted_route: Dynamic column discovery (strip whitespace, exclude 'Right AUX'), pd.to_datetime(float_series, unit='s', utc=True), 60s baseline, bp.protocols.CFT try/except fallback
rounds_summary.observation: OutOfBoundsDatetime: timestamp value ~4.98e13 overflows nanosecond int64; pd.to_datetime with unit='s' on that float exceeds pandas M8[ns] bounds

rounds_summary.attempted_route: Same dynamic column discovery, cast timestamp to np.int64 before pd.to_datetime(unit='s', utc=True) to remove sub-second float precision; dropped biopsykit dependency
rounds_summary.observation: Same OutOfBoundsDatetime crash at identical traceback line — int64 cast does not fix overflow because the raw value ~4.98e13 is still out of range for seconds-unit nanosecond datetime

ruled_out_routes: Hard-coded column names ['TP9','AF7','AF8','TP10'] with pd.read_excel — actual sheet headers differ
pd.to_datetime(float_series, unit='s', utc=True) on raw timestamp column — overflows nanosecond dtype
pd.to_datetime(ts.astype(np.int64), unit='s', utc=True) — int64 cast does not prevent OutOfBoundsDatetime when raw values are ~4.98e13
bp.protocols.CFT() instantiation as calculation driver — never reached due to timestamp crash in rounds 2-3
content.synthesised_diagnosis: All three rounds failed because the 'timestamps' column in MIST3 is NOT Unix seconds: its raw values (~4.98e13) are approximately 50 billion seconds, which overflows pandas nanosecond datetime. The values are consistent with Unix milliseconds (~year 2000). Round 1 never reached timestamp parsing (column name mismatch); rounds 2-3 fixed that but both crashed on the same overflow because neither tried unit='ms' or a non-datetime numeric index.
content.rationale: The timestamp magnitude ~4.98e10 (after /1000) fits Unix seconds for year 2000-era data, confirming millisecond encoding. Every crash traces to assuming unit='s'; the CFT logic itself (mean baseline, min onset, percent change) was structurally sound in all rounds.
content.recommended_alternative: After pd.read_excel with dynamic column discovery and whitespace stripping, parse timestamps as milliseconds: use pd.to_datetime(pd.to_numeric(df[timestamp_col], errors='coerce'), unit='ms', utc=True). If that still overflows, fall back to a plain numeric offset index: subtract the first timestamp value and work in seconds arithmetically (no DatetimeIndex needed). Then apply the existing 60s baseline/min-onset CFT formula unchanged.
